All right, welcome everyone. This is Total Breakthrough Training. Today we're going to be talking about the seven master steps. And the seven master steps are one of the main templates that we use to teach the process of creating lasting change in people. Even though we call it the seven master steps, it's not a real sequential step-by-step necessarily process. It's more like a checklist of seven things that are important for someone to experience or for the interventionist to pay attention to as they create change in the person for that change to last and becomes something that they take home and it becomes a robust kind of change. So Master Unit 7 was a selection of seven intervention clubs from different breakthrough interventions. And so I'm going to walk you through the seven steps and how they work. The first one is understanding and appreciating the person's world. Now the first step to understanding a client is always understanding and appreciating them. The coaching is based on the client's interest in creating change, which means that you need to align with their interest and their desire to change. In order to align with their desire to change, you need to understand them. The best, most helpful way to understand them is to try to really understand everything they have going on, the relationships and everything they have going for them, all their resources as well. Take an inventory of all their resources so that you can help them create the change that they want. Now if you were to say, what's the worst kind of coaching? The worst kind of coaching is kind of like the worst kind of therapy or the worst kind of doctor diagnosis. So where the expert makes their judgment and creates a diagnosis based on their own criteria and their own priorities and they categorize a person's life or behavior or identity according to their own system. So that's where if someone tells you, you know, the old school psychoanalysts would tell you they had an editable complex and you had sexual thoughts or your parents. And these are cliches, but the cliches speak to something. The doctor who goes ahead and prescribes you an anti-depressant based on a five-minute conversation. And that happens. That happens really easily. So coaching where you want to be careful is that you don't just create judgments or impose your own framework on the person without really truly understanding and meeting, standing in their shoes and understanding where they're coming from. So the best kind of intervention is the one where we enter the world of your client, you understand their desires, their outcomes, their relationships, their conflicts, their resources, and you appreciate them from a place of empathy. So the second step is leverage. Create a relationship where you have leverage. Obviously the opposite of leverage is creating a relationship where you have no leverage, meaning you have no agency, you don't have influence. Now leverage refers, it's the next step of understanding because it ensures that you not only understand the person but they understand them. And that they are associated to the situation, the consequences that they're trying to avoid or achieve. So often what happens in life is people get habituated to problems. They get used to losing money or not having money and they forget that they might lose a lot more money by doing that. So leverage is there to help people associate to the importance of taking action. So leverage has a hard sound to it. It sounds like you're going to put a crowbar to something. And it's used in business and in kind of forcing situations like when you leverage buyout, meaning it's against someone's wishes that you buy their company. That's not what we mean. So we differentiate between hard leverage and soft leverage. Now hard leverage would be literally forcing someone to do something and there is a tradition of very unskillful interventions, not strategic interventions where people will, for instance, surround someone and confront them. And that's like we're forcing you, we're making you do it. You have no choice, you have to follow, you have to comply. The problem with hard level leverage, especially in coaching, is that the client has a lot of control in relation to the coach. They don't like you, they don't feel like you don't get it, they feel like you're not smart, they feel like you don't have good intentions, they feel like you got it wrong, they can just decide not to cooperate with you. And so hard leverage, if it's ever used, and we really kind of don't advocate people to use it unless you're very, very advanced, it has to be handled with very nimble fingers, very adroitly. So it's a way of helping to make sure that the change is robust and that the person has practiced and exercised the process of raising themselves up and solving the problem again. And for the seventh step, it's helping the person relate to their decision to their high schools and most important relationships so that their decisions will last and improve every aspect of their life. So when people make a new change or a new decision, it's really important to reward it heavily and to integrate it and hook it up to as many things that are meaningful in that person's life as possible. So that's, you know, Tony will often use the stage and people will applaud and get hugs and stuff. And that is one way to get people really associated. In Breakthrough, he'll use situations of victory and achievement and overcoming an ordeal. But it's also just about really talking about all the good that's come from the change. So it can be very simple. It can be talking about the consequences like now that I've decided to be XYZ, I can see all the effects it's going to have on PDQ. And so this is where you want to go back to step one and think of all the relationships and all the things that were important to the person and all their goals and all their values and hook up the new change to all those things. Hook it up meaning reflect on how it's going to impact all those things positively so that the person feels whole and they feel like it's a part of their whole part of their life. So that's the Seven Master Steps. It was kind of a lengthy description. I hope they're helpful to you. Anyone has any questions of any type? We'd love to answer them. Press star two. And again, the Seven Master Steps are not a recipe or a step-by-step kind of thing. It's more like seven principles of change that we try to pay attention to. So sometimes when we go back and we try to map the Seven Master Steps and they give an intervention, whether it's by Tony or Magalie or Chloe or myself or someone else, we'll often say, okay, so which step is this? And we'll find that one thing accomplished three steps. Or you'll find that Tony or someone is working with someone and then they discover a whole new situation. So for instance, he's working with Lisa and her father in the memories of her father and then he discovers that she doesn't speak to her sister and says, oh, wow, okay, that's a whole other loop. That's a whole other relationship. So back to the Seven Master Steps, going back to understanding that part of the person's life and now getting leverage in that part so that they understand how it's related to everything. And then what patterns need to be interrupted in that area? And then how do you redefine that problem? And then what are some creative options that you can offer them for that? And then how can they condition that? So it can be applied to any new major area or discovery of the person's life or another area, a new problem that comes up to the front. I always give people too much to do in the week, hoping that they will do one of the things. There's always an action that I'm asking someone to take, and it could either be thinking a certain way, calling someone. And usually it relates to both. So an example, I was talking to a woman last week who felt a lot of self-judgment and frustration about her critical nature. So I asked her to spend five minutes a day thinking about her mother and allowing herself to say something to herself to give herself advice as if she was her mother. Even though we both knew it was not actually the mother. It was just that part of her that she could be attuned to that was more like her mother, less judgmental and critical. And then I asked her to also tell her mother that she was doing this exercise. So she was actually doing something in real time, and I always asked the client to tell their spouse, tell their children, to let the coaching action be public. Because the point where somebody doesn't believe that they can take an action, or it's going to make a difference, which is usually where they're at. You know, they feel stuck. So you're getting them out of being stuck where they can take some real action. Is there a rule of thumb for what you would prescribe to them to actually get them to do it so they don't come back telling you that they didn't follow up on it, or that they just couldn't hold that momentum? And try to make it as easy and brief as possible. Or it's something that you only have to do once during the week, and it's something that could be possibly completely ridiculous. Never something that would be very hard. And if there's someone who's really stuck who won't do it away from me, then we just do it together. Right. And I've done that too. That's not usually the problem I have as a coach. I usually have clients who are incredibly driven to take actions. Okay. Or ask them to call and decide what three people they're going to call and ask these three questions.
